Page 3, Introduction (end of discussion of Universal Portfolios and computational barriers).

Meanwhile, no algorithm except Universal Portfolios is known to admit a regret bound matching (5) up to a constant factor. In other words, the following problem stands:
Propose a computationally feasible and near-optimal online portfolio selection algorithm.
title: Design practical regret-optimal online portfolio selection algorithms without high-dimensional integration
background_latex:
Consider the online portfolio selection game over a horizon \(T\in\mathbb{N}\) with \(d\ge 2\) assets.

1) Decision set: the simplex \(\Delta_d:=\{w\in\mathbb{R}^d_+:\sum_{i=1}^d w[i]=1\}\).

2) Adversary’s move: at each round \(t\in\{1,\dots,T\}\), after observing the learner’s choice \(w_t\in\Delta_d\), the adversary reveals a nonnegative return vector \(x_t\in\mathbb{R}^d_+\).

3) Loss: the learner suffers the logarithmic loss
\[
\ell_t(w):=-\log(x_t^\top w),\qquad w\in\Delta_d.
\]

4) Comparator class: constantly rebalanced portfolios (CRP), i.e. fixed \(w\in\Delta_d\) used in every round.

5) Regret against the best CRP in hindsight:
\[
R_T(w_{1:T}\mid x_{1:T}) := \sum_{t=1}^T \ell_t(w_t)\; -\; \min_{w\in\Delta_d}\sum_{t=1}^T \ell_t(w).
\]

Cover’s Universal Portfolios strategy selects at time \(t\) a portfolio equal to the expectation under a density \(\phi_t\) on \(\Delta_d\) proportional to \(\exp\big(-\sum_{\tau=1}^{t-1} \ell_\tau(w)\big)\), which yields regret \((d-1)\log(T+1)\) for all sequences \(x_{1:T}\). However, this update requires (approximately) computing a \(d\)-dimensional integral (the expectation under \(\phi_t\)), which is computationally expensive.

An algorithm is called computationally feasible here if its per-round update can be implemented in time polynomial in \(d\) and \(T\) with reasonable dependence (e.g. nearly quadratic in \(d\)), and without requiring high-dimensional numerical integration or expensive sampling from \(\phi_t\) at each round.
problem_definition_latex:
Construct an online portfolio selection algorithm (a mapping from past observations to \(w_t\in\Delta_d\)) such that:

1) For every horizon \(T\) and every adversarial sequence \(x_{1:T}\in(\mathbb{R}^d_+)^T\), the regret satisfies
\[
R_T(w_{1:T}\mid x_{1:T}) = O\big(d\log T\big)
\]
(up to universal constant factors, matching the \(\Theta(d\log T)\) worst-case optimal rate of Universal Portfolios).

2) Each round’s update is computationally feasible in the sense of avoiding explicit high-dimensional integration over \(\Delta_d\) (as required by Universal Portfolios) and admitting an efficient implementation with polynomial-time per-round complexity in \(d\) and \(T\).
latex:
Consider the online portfolio selection game over a horizon \(T\in\mathbb{N}\) with \(d\ge 2\) assets.

1) Decision set: the simplex \(\Delta_d:=\{w\in\mathbb{R}^d_+:\sum_{i=1}^d w[i]=1\}\).

2) Adversary’s move: at each round \(t\in\{1,\dots,T\}\), after observing the learner’s choice \(w_t\in\Delta_d\), the adversary reveals a nonnegative return vector \(x_t\in\mathbb{R}^d_+\).

3) Loss: the learner suffers the logarithmic loss
\[
\ell_t(w):=-\log(x_t^\top w),\qquad w\in\Delta_d.
\]

4) Comparator class: constantly rebalanced portfolios (CRP), i.e. fixed \(w\in\Delta_d\) used in every round.

5) Regret against the best CRP in hindsight:
\[
R_T(w_{1:T}\mid x_{1:T}) := \sum_{t=1}^T \ell_t(w_t)\; -\; \min_{w\in\Delta_d}\sum_{t=1}^T \ell_t(w).
\]

Cover’s Universal Portfolios strategy selects at time \(t\) a portfolio equal to the expectation under a density \(\phi_t\) on \(\Delta_d\) proportional to \(\exp\big(-\sum_{\tau=1}^{t-1} \ell_\tau(w)\big)\), which yields regret \((d-1)\log(T+1)\) for all sequences \(x_{1:T}\). However, this update requires (approximately) computing a \(d\)-dimensional integral (the expectation under \(\phi_t\)), which is computationally expensive.

An algorithm is called computationally feasible here if its per-round update can be implemented in time polynomial in \(d\) and \(T\) with reasonable dependence (e.g. nearly quadratic in \(d\)), and without requiring high-dimensional numerical integration or expensive sampling from \(\phi_t\) at each round.

Construct an online portfolio selection algorithm (a mapping from past observations to \(w_t\in\Delta_d\)) such that:

1) For every horizon \(T\) and every adversarial sequence \(x_{1:T}\in(\mathbb{R}^d_+)^T\), the regret satisfies
\[
R_T(w_{1:T}\mid x_{1:T}) = O\big(d\log T\big)
\]
(up to universal constant factors, matching the \(\Theta(d\log T)\) worst-case optimal rate of Universal Portfolios).

2) Each round’s update is computationally feasible in the sense of avoiding explicit high-dimensional integration over \(\Delta_d\) (as required by Universal Portfolios) and admitting an efficient implementation with polynomial-time per-round complexity in \(d\) and \(T\).
location_in_paper: Page 3, Introduction (end of discussion of Universal Portfolios and computational barriers).
open_problem_quote:
Meanwhile, no algorithm except Universal Portfolios is known to admit a regret bound matching (5) up to a constant factor. In other words, the following problem stands:
Propose a computationally feasible and near-optimal online portfolio selection algorithm.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is existential/constructive (design an algorithm) rather than a universal inequality that might be falsified by a finite counterexample instance search. Automated instance generation is unlikely to directly yield progress, since failure depends on worst-case regret over all sequences.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize candidate online algorithms (e.g., FTRL/OMD-style with tunable regularizers, update rules, and approximations) and score them via simulated regret on adversarially generated markets. However, the fitness signal (worst-case regret) is hard to approximate reliably, and proving an \(O(d\log T)\) guarantee would still require substantial analytical work.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
